coredata.dc:title: The construction of white-light-emitting anisotropic hydrogel
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Guizhen
Niu, Qingyu
Liu, Xiao
He, Liang
Yu, Jinxie
Han, Hang
Wang, Wenjing
Zhang, Ying
Li, Zhiqiang
Xiong, Qingqing
coredata.dc:description: In this work, hybrid anisotropic hydrogel is developed through dispersing carbon quantum dots, ultrahigh magnetic nanoparticle-coated alumina (Fe/Al2O3) platelets in acrylamide aqueous solution in the assistance of magnetic field, then fix the oriented structure via gelation. The aligned Fe/Al2O3 platelets result in formation of anisotropic structure, thus endows the hydrogels mechanical anisotropy and fluorescent anisotropy. White-light emission is obtained via varying the weight ratio of yellow-emitting ZnO QDs and blue-emitting N,S-CDs. This strategy not only enriches the mechanical/fluorescent dual anisotropic soft materials, but also have great potential application in the fields of organic light-emitting diodes, nonlinear optics and field effect transistors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: White-light-emitting hydrogel
Carbon dots
Quantum dots
Anisotropic
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Anisotropic mechanical properties of the hydrogel
Anisotropic fluorescent properties of the hydrogel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) SEM images of anisotropic hydrogels parallel (∥), perpendicular (⊥) to the magnetic field direction and isotropic hydrogel. (D–F) Schematic of anisotropic and isotropic hydrogels in different direction.
(A) Tensile stress–strain curves, (B) compressive stress–strain curves and (C) rheological properties of the hybrid hydrogel. (D–F) And the corresponding schematic.

(A) Picture of fluorescent hydrogels by addition the different weight ratio ZnO QDs and N,S-CDs (from left to right), and corresponding (B) emission spectra, (C) CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram of hydrogels. (λ ex = 365 nm).

(A) Emission and (B) excitation spectra of white-light-emitting hydrogels in different direction. (C–D) Schematic of white-light-emitting anisotropic hydrogels in parallel, perpendicular direction and (E) white-light-emitting isotropic hydrogels in random direction irradiation. (λ ex = 365 nm).
Schematic illustration for the construction of white-light-emitting anisotropic hydrogel.

head.ce:title: The construction of white-light-emitting anisotropic hydrogel

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Hepatobiliary Cancer, Liver Cancer Center, Tianjin Medical University Cancer Institute & Hospital, National Clinical Research Center for Cancer, Key Laboratory of Cancer Prevention and Therapy, Tianjin's Clinical Research Center for Cancer, Tianjin 300060, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, hybrid anisotropic hydrogel is developed through dispersing carbon quantum dots, ultrahigh magnetic nanoparticle-coated alumina (Fe/Al2O3) platelets in acrylamide aqueous solution in the assistance of magnetic field, then fix the oriented structure via gelation. The aligned Fe/Al2O3 platelets result in formation of anisotropic structure, thus endows the hydrogels mechanical anisotropy and fluorescent anisotropy. White-light emission is obtained via varying the weight ratio of yellow-emitting ZnO QDs and blue-emitting N,S-CDs. This strategy not only enriches the mechanical/fluorescent dual anisotropic soft materials, but also have great potential application in the fields of organic light-emitting diodes, nonlinear optics and field effect transistors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: White-light-emitting hydrogels have attracted significant interest in the field of display and environment lighting [1,2]. In general, white-light-emitting hydrogels were prepared by incorporating three primary colors (red-green-blue) or two complementary colors (yellow-blue) [3–5]. Lanthanide ions (Ln3+), organic fluorescent dyes, quantum dots and carbon dots were used as emitting sources in gel matrices [6–11]. Comparing with traditional fluorescence center, quantum dots and carbon dots have many advantages, such as low toxicity, good water solubility, tunable emission color and superior fluorescent stability [12–16]. For example, Xu et al. reported machine learning guided full-color V4C3 MXene quantum dots to construct white light emitting diodes (WLEDs) [17]. M. Shet et al. treated the surface of Mn2+-doped ZnS quantum dot (orange, QD) with cholinetosylate ionic liquid (blue, IL), leading to formation of white light emitting composite (WLE QD-IL) [18].

On the other hand, hydrogels have been widely used in tissue engineering due to their good biocompatibility [19]. Many tissues in human have high ordered hierarchical structure, which are essential for the to carry out special function. Hence, anisotropic hydrogel is promise scaffold in tissue engineering [20,21]. Synthetic anisotropic hydrogels are usually prepared via aligning nanofillers, polymer networks and pores under electrospinning [22–24], magnetic fields [25–28], electric fields [29–32], mechanical stretching and shear forces [33–35], unidirectional freezing [36–38], and fixing the hierarchical structures by gelation. Specific, magnetic fields are used to preparate anisotropic hydrogels in a noncontact manner [39]. We have been engaged in the development of lanthanide-containing anisotropic hydrogels with both mechanical properties and fluorescent behaviors through the alignment of ultrahigh magnetic nanoparticle-coated alumina (Fe/Al2O3) platelets in magnetic field, however, white-light-emitting anisotropic hydrogel has never been realized [40,41]. We thus wonder whether this strategy is applicable to other fluorophore to obtain anisotropic fluorescent hydrogel with wider spectral tunability.

To verify the versatility of this strategy, we here develop white-light-emitting anisotropic hydrogel, which is constructed by fixing two complementary emitters (yellow-emitting ZnO QDs and blue-emitting N,S-CDs), aligned Fe/Al2O3 platelets into polymeric hydrogel network by employing magnetic field (Scheme 1 ). The alignment of Fe/Al2O3 platelets improve the mechanical properties in an anisotropic manner, thus render the obtained hybrid hydrogel anisotropic mechanical performance. Additionally, the direction-dependent shading effect of the oriented Fe/Al2O3 platelets on emitters also endows the hydrogel anisotropic fluorescent properties. White-light-emitting hydrogel is further achieved through adjusting the weight ratio of the two fluorescent emitting centers. Comparing with other WLEDs, the hydrogel we report here shows both anisotropic structure and anisotropic fluorescent properties, which have great potentials in anisotropic sensing and physiological signal transduction.

Alumina platelets (Al2O3 platelets, diameter is 8.5 μm, thickness is 250 nm) was obtained from Merck. Ultrahigh magnetic nanoparticle (EMG-705) was purchased from Ferrotec, Germany. N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA), Citric acid, Zinc aetate dihydrate (Zn(OAc)2·2H2O), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), and ammonium persulfate (APS) were obtained from Aladdin, Shanghai, China. Acrylamide (AAm), methyl cellulose (MC) and N, N, N′, N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TMEDA) were procured from J&K Scientific, Beijing, China. l-cysteine was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Fengchuan Chemical Reagent, Tianjin, China. Ethanol was purchased from Chemical Reagent Factory No.6, Tianjin, China. The external magnetic field (PEM-60, 300 mT) was obtained from Tianjie Magnetoelectrian, Mianyang, China.

The morphology of hydrogel was examined with TESCAN GAlA3, with an acceleration voltage of 10 kV. The sample of hydrogel was lyophilized in a freeze dryer and sputter-coated with platinum before test. The tensile and compressive properties of hydrogels were determined on CMT6104 with a load cell of 500 N. The tensile sample of hydrogels were prepared as strip shape (20 mm × 20 mm × 20 mm), and measurements were carried out when the crosshead speed of 100 mm min−1. The compressive sample of hydrogels were prepared as square shape (10 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm), and the loading rate was 4 mm min−1. Rheological tests of hydrogels were carried out by using an Anton Paar model MCR-301 rheometer, with a 25 mm diameter parallel plate attached to a transducer. All experiments were measured at 25 °C. The fluorescent properties of hydrogel were measured on Edinburgh Instruments FS920P near-infrared spectrometer. The UV spectrum was obtained on an Agilent Carry 100 UV spectrometer, the range from 200 to 450 nm.

The Fe/Al2O3 platelets were prepared according to the reported method [42]. ZnO quantum dots (ZnO QDs) was prepared according to the previous report method [43], and N,S-carbon dots (N,S-CDs) was prepared according to pervious report method [44–46]. The aqueous solution of 700 μL 1.2 wt% ZnO QDs and 500 μL 0.05 wt% N, S-CDs were mixed homogeneously under stirring. Acrylamide (AAm 250 mg) was dissolved in the above solution. Then, thickening agent MC (250 μL 2.5 wt%) aqueous solution, 45 mg Fe/Al2O3 platelets and cross-linker MBAA (30 μL 2.5 wt%) aqueous solution were added. Finally, initiator APS (10 μL 7 wt%) aqueous solution, and accelerator TMEDA (7 μL 30 wt%) aqueous solution were added. The mixed solution was immediately transferred into polytetrafluoroethylene mold (10 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm) and applied in magnetic field at room temperature for about 12 h to initiate copolymerization, affording the hybrid hydrogel. Hybrid hydrogels were prepared with increasing the content of Fe/Al2O3 platelets from 0 wt% to 5 wt% with same method. The tensile strength of the hydrogel significantly improved with the increase of Fe/Al2O3 platelets content, however, the fluorescence of the hydrogel decreased with the increase of Fe/Al2O3 platelets (Fig. S1). Hence, the content of Fe/Al2O3 platelets was fixed at 3 wt%.

The highly ordered hierarchical structure of anisotropic hydrogel was confirmed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Edges of Fe/Al2O3 platelets were observed parallel to the direction of the magnetic field (Fig. 1 A). Planes of Fe/Al2O3 platelets were seen clearly along the direction perpendicular to the magnetic field (Fig. 1B). By contrast, the isotropic hydrogels prepared without magnetic field showed randomly distributed Fe/Al2O3 platelets (Fig. 1C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anisotropic mechanical properties of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The anisotropic structure should bring direction-dependent mechanical performance to the hybrid hydrogel, that means the mechanical properties will strongly depend on the loading direction. The stress of the anisotropic hydrogel was dramatically improved compared to the hydrogel without Fe/Al2O3 platelets (Fig. S1A). The stress (σ T∥) of parallel to the direction of the magnetic field was reached to be 60.14 kPa whose extension ratio (λ ∥) was 5.0 (Fig. 2 A), while these values perpendicular to the magnetic field was much lower (σ T⟂ = 28.86 kPa, λ ⟂ = 3.8). The stress of isotropic hydrogel was in between σ T∥ and σ T⟂. The tensile anisotropic parameter (defined as the gap between parallel and perpendicular direction) was 2.1 (Eq. S1(1)). The results demonstrated the alignment of Fe/Al2O3 platelets worked as the crack bridges, resisted crack prolongation and dissipated mechanical energy with the stretching process, therefore protected the hydrogel from further damage (Scheme S1) [47]. In compression tests, the hydrogel exhibited compressive stress (σ C∥) of 802.49 kPa when compression parallel to direction of the magnetic field, and that value was 1.8 times (Eq. S1(2)) larger than that of the perpendicular direction (σ C⟂ = 443.41 kPa, Fig. 2B). This was because compression parallel to Fe/Al2O3 platelets required to withstand greater forces from the bending and breaking of the Fe/Al2O3 platelets, while compression perpendicular to the direction of the Fe/Al2O3 platelets primarily mainly loaded on the polymeric network (Scheme S2) [40,41,48]. In rheological tests, the storage modulus G′ was 1226 Pa and 2486 Pa in both parallel and perpendicular directions, respectively, and the corresponding rheological anisotropic parameter was 2.0 (Fig. 2C, Eq. S1(3)) [49]. Therefore, anisotropic structure contributed to the anisotropic mechanical properties in the hybrid hydrogel.

Individual ZnO QDs showed an emission peak at 539 nm upon excitation of 369 nm in aqueous solution, and absorption peak matched with excitation (Fig. S4A and Fig. S5A) [43]. The aqueous solution of N,S-CDs on excitation with a radiation of 305, 380 nm emitted at 418 nm, and absorption peaked at 343 nm (Fig. S4B and Fig. S5B) [44–46]. The fluorescent behavior of both ZnO QDs and N,S-CDs show good water stability [43–46]. Then, a series of hydrogels were prepared with a fixed content of Fe/Al2O3 platelets (3 wt%), and different amount of ZnO QDs and N,S-CDs at room temperature (Fig. 3 ), the emission color varied from yellow to blue via white zone when excited at 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3A and B, Table S1). When the weight ratio of ZnO QDs and N,S-CDs was 33.6:1, white-light emission could be observed from the hydrogel with the CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates of (0.28, 0.31), which is far from the ideal white light chromaticity coordinates (0.33, 0.33). We believe this is because the presence of yellow Fe/Al2O3 platelets. To confirm our hypothesis, we further investigated the fluorescent properties of the mixed aqueous solution of carbon quantum dots without Fe/Al2O3 platelets. When the weight ratio of ZnO QDs and N,S-CDs was 168.0:1, white-light-emitting aqueous solution was obtained, however, the emitting color was yellow at this ratio in the presence of Fe/Al2O3 platelets (Fig. S7A). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity decreased with the addition of Fe/Al2O3 platelets (Fig. S7B and Fig. S7D, Fig. S7C and Fig. S7E). Thus, the scattering originated from opaque Fe/Al2O3 platelets likely reduced the penetration of photons into the yellow matrix, resulting in emitting color difference from transparent hydrogel and reduced emission intensity of the two emitters in hydrogel [50,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anisotropic fluorescent properties of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: In additional to the anisotropic mechanical properties, the white-light-emitting hydrogel also demonstrated remarkable anisotropic fluorescence properties. Upon excitation at 365 nm, the two emission peaks located at 418 nm and 539 nm, respectively, belong to the emission peaks of N, S- CDs and ZnO QDs (Fig. S6). Compared to that of perpendicular excitation (I em⟂) and isotropic hydrogels, the emission intensity (I em∥) of white-light-emitting hydrogel was significantly stronger when excited parallel to the direction of the magnetic field (Fig. 4 A and Fig. 4B), with fluorescent anisotropic parameter (I em∥/I em⟂) of 1.8 (Eq. S1(4)). While the fluorescence lifetime (τ) and quantum efficiency (Φ) did not show direction-dependent behavior (Fig. S8 and Table S4), thus, the fluorescent anisotropy came from the anisotropic structure. After leaving the anisotropic hydrogels for one week, no obvious fluorescence quenching was observed, and the fluorescent anisotropy was also maintained (Fig. S9), implying the anisotropic hydrogel is stable. When the excitation was applied parallel to the direction of the magnetic field, the non-emissive absorption of Fe/Al2O3 platelets was minimum, since it came from the edges of Fe/Al2O3 platelets, leading to weaker shading effect of the oriented Fe/Al2O3 platelet on the emitting source (Fig. 4C). On the contrary, the Fe/Al2O3 platelets showed maximum absorption along the perpendicular direction, displaying the stronger shading effect and decreasing the emission (Fig. 4D). This fluorescence anisotropic behavior of white-light-emitting hydrogel did not originate from the emitting center itself but from highly ordered arrangement of Fe/Al2O3 platelets [52].

In summary, we reported a white-light-emitting anisotropic hydrogel by fixing magnetically aligned Fe/Al2O3 platelets, QDs and CDs into polymeric matrix. The hydrogel showed anisotropic mechanical strength, because the alignment of Fe/Al2O3 platelets reinforced the hydrogel in an anisotropic manner, and the direction-dependent shading effect the oriented of Fe/Al2O3 platelets on the emitting centers, and enable the hydrogels anisotropic fluorescent behaviors. White-light-emitting hydrogel was further obtained by varying the ratio of the two complementary emitters. These results demonstrate our strategy is applicable to diverse fluorophore, and will enrich the emitting range and tunability of mechanical/fluorescent dual anisotropic hydrogels, which have great potentials in anisotropic sensing and physiological signal transduction.

Guizhen Yu: designed the experiments, Writing – original draft, drafted the manuscript, performed the experiments. Qingyu Niu: performed the experiments. Xiao Liu: performed the experiments. Liang He: discussed the experimental data with Z. Li. Jinxie Yu: performed the experiments. Hang Han: performed the experiments. Wenjing Wang: performed the experiments. Ying Zhang: Data curation, discussed the experimental data with Z. Li. Zhiqiang Li: designed the experiments, Supervision, supervised the work and edited the manuscript. Qingqing Xiong: Supervision, supervised the work and edited the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22171069 and 21871075), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (B2021202026), the Program for Top 100 Innovative Talents in Colleges and Universities of Hebei Province (SLRC2019023).
